details.calculation: ($1,285,280 / 3,124 total beneficiaries) = $411.42 per outcome. | A $1,000 donation achieves ≈ 2.43 outcomes.

metrics.context_qualifier: Total wild animals rescued in Singapore during FY 2023-24, including native and exotic species from illegal trade or distress.
Native wild animals successfully released back into their natural habitats in Singapore during FY 2023-24.
metrics.evidence_quote: More than 1,040 native wild animals were successfully released back into their natural habitats and three exotic wild reptiles were repatriated to the wild this financial year.

metrics.context_qualifier: Animals rescued from glue traps in Singapore during FY 2023-24, including reptiles, birds, and mammals. This is a subset of total rescues.
metrics.evidence_quote: In this financial year, the total number of animals rescued from glue traps in Singapore, by ACRES is 242.

metrics.context_qualifier: Reptiles repatriated from illegal wildlife trade in Singapore during FY 2023-24. This is a subset of total rescues.
metrics.evidence_quote: ACRES repatriated 3 reptiles from the illegal wildlife trade, in this financial year, on 18th July 2023, scoring another victory for wildlife against the cruel and wasteful illegal wildlife trade.

metrics.context_qualifier: Calls handled by ACRES's 24-hour wildlife rescue hotline in Singapore during FY 2023-24.
metrics.evidence_quote: From 1st April 2023 to 31st March 2024, our 24-hour hotline (97837782) received 14,447 calls from members of the public.

metrics.context_qualifier: Individuals reached through education programs, roadshows, and talks in Singapore during FY 2023-24.
Cases/emails related to human-wildlife conflicts and interactions handled by ACRES in Singapore during FY 2023-24.
metrics.evidence_quote: ACRES team also has advised a total of 721 calls/cases/emails related to feedback on human-wildlife conflicts and interactions.

metrics.context_qualifier: Cases investigated by the Animal Crime Investigation Unit (ACIU) related to animal cruelty and illegal wildlife trade in Singapore during FY 2023-24.
metrics.evidence_quote: This financial year, our ACIU (Animal Crime Investigation Unit) investigated a total of 179 cases related to animal cruelty and illegal wildlife trade

significant_events.source_quote: In 2009, we established Singapore's first dedicated facility to house rescued and trafficked wildlife. The ACRES Wildlife Rescue Centre (AWRC) educates the public, rescues, treats and rehabilitates hundreds of wild animals every year, some from the illegal exotic pet trade and others native to the forests in Singapore that are found injured, lost or disorientated. We run our wildlife rescue services 24 hours a day (as of 31 Mar 2024), 365 days a year.
significant_events.summary: ACRES operates a 24-hour wildlife rescue service, providing safe refuge, rehabilitation, and release/repatriation for wild animals in distress or from illegal trade in Singapore.

significant_events.source_quote: The illegal wildlife trade subjects billions of animals to pain and suffering, and threatens to wipe out countless species. At ACRES, we combat this wildlife trade through our 24-hour Wildlife Crime Hotline, undercover investigations, public awareness campaigns and collaborations with other NGOs and the government. Our investigations have led to the capture of many wildlife criminals. Our campaigns and engagements with the government have led to vast improvements to national wildlife protection laws.
significant_events.summary: ACRES combats illegal wildlife trade through undercover investigations, public awareness campaigns, and collaborations with authorities to improve national wildlife protection laws.

significant_events.event_name: Advocacy for Changes to Glue Traps
significant_events.source_quote: Singapore's first public perception survey on glue traps : Most people view rats and mice as pests, associating them with filth, crop destruction, and disease, leading to common rodent control methods like poisons, live traps, and glue traps. Glue traps, however, cause prolonged suffering and are indiscriminate in what they catch. Despite this, there are no local regulations governing their sale or use, raising concerns about the humane treatment of trapped animals. Glue traps are favored for their convenience, low cost, and misleading marketing as “clean and harmless.” To better understand public perceptions and biases towards animals considered pests, ACRES, which has been rescuing wildlife from glue traps, partnered with Antzworkz Consultants and Rakuten in November 2023 to explore awareness and attitudes towards these traps.
significant_events.summary: ACRES advocates for a ban on glue traps, highlighting their cruelty and impact on wildlife, and engaging stakeholders for policy changes.

significant_events.source_quote: In April 2022, Singapore's Urban Wildlife Working Group members together with other partners in the nature community launched the Our Wild Neighbours (OWN) initiative - a consolidated, country-wide outreach programme to promote coexistence with Singapore's native wildlife.
significant_events.summary: A multi-stakeholder outreach program promoting human-wildlife coexistence in Singapore through education, advisories, and community engagement.

significant_events.source_quote: We never forget that the key to ending animal abuse is education. Our talks, roadshows and Humane Education programmes touch a wide cross section of society, from children in kindergarten to the elderly in the heartland, reaching over 20,000 individuals each year. We travel to schools as well as malls, both downtown and in the heartland. Our aim is to encourage sympathy, compassion and respect for animals as well as other human beings, and to inspire and empower children to change their world for the better.
significant_events.summary: ACRES delivers educational programs, roadshows, and talks to students and individuals to promote animal welfare and cruelty-free living.

data.original_text: ACRES is an animal protection organisation, driven by our concern for animals.
We adopt research projects on the use of animals in various fields.

Research findings are then used to educate the public to promote active community involvement in the animal protection movement, as well as strive towards synergistic partnerships with authorities and related parties.
Wildlife Rescue: ACRES rescues any wild animals in need of help in Singapore.

This includes native wild animals (such as pythons, monitor lizards, civets and pangolins) and also exotic animals who have been illegally brought into Singapore to be illegally sold as pets, who may be handed over to us by their owners, or may be found abandoned.
To educate people on lifestyle choices which do not involve the abuse of animals and which are environment-friendly.
ACRES (Animal Concerns Research and Education Society), is a registered charity and Institution of Public Character (IPC), founded by Singaporeans in 2001.

Our journey began with campaigning for wildlife in captivity and to end the illegal wildlife trade in Singapore, and subsequently spearheaded campaigns that influenced policy changes, reshaped societal attitudes, and directly improved the lives of countless animals through our wildlife rescue, rehabilitation, release and repatriation efforts.
We made strides in creating a more compassionate Singapore, positively impacting the lives of animals by the food we eat, products we buy, and our treatment towards animals.
ACRES's mission extends beyond conservation. We are dedicated to promoting animal welfare and advocating for the rights of all animals.

In the 2019 financial year (ending 31st March 2019), our amazing volunteers clocked over 18,300 hours; our education team were directly involved with 72 student projects, and reached out to over 42,000 individuals, through over 140 roadshows and talks; our rescue team handled 9,811 calls to our hotline, leading to rescue of over 3,300 wild animals in distress.

Our Animal Crime Investigation Unit (ACIU) handled over 230 cases related to wildlife trade and cruelty and the wildlife management team engaged community through over 100 cases of human-wildlife conflict in Singapore.
ACRES is a local charity founded by Singaporeans in 2001. Our sole aim is to advocate for an end to animal cruelty in Asia.
We tackle wildlife crime in Singapore via our ACRES Crime Investigation Unit.
We operate a 24-hour wildlife rescue service, which has rescued and relocated more than 10000 injured or displaced animals to date.
We have also conducted over 2700 outreach programmes in the past...

Each month, ACRES: Handles 1,200 wildlife rescue hotline calls. Rescues over 260 wild animals. Cares for 120 animals rescued from illegal wildlife trade. Manages over 52 animal human-wildlife interactions. Educates and empowers over 1,000 individuals.
In 2023 alone, we reached over 32,550 people through our wildlife rescue efforts, humane education programs, and advocacy for animal welfare.
ACRES gets approval to use its original land that was previously contaminated by the contractor.

ACRES rescues, rehabilitates and releases Oil-spilled kingfisher; On 14 June 2024, an oil spill in Sentosa made an impact on Singapore's wildlife. Two oil-drenched kingfishers were rescued, rehabilitated, and released.
The OWN Travelling Roadshow Library Tour visited seven libraries across Singapore over four months, engaging close to 5,000 people through public programmes.
Two turtles, Genie the Giant Asian pond turtle and Jasmine the Malaysian giant turtle, were rescued, housed, and eventually repatriated to Malaysia.
ACRES received the Municipal Services Award for their efforts in managing and implementing the Otter-Proofing for Coexistence project in Seletar Hills Estate.
ACRES hand-raised its first raptor, a Sunda scops owl.
The first joint animal advisories for commonly encountered wild animals was released for the public and is available online for download on Our Wild Neighbours (OWN) website.
A series of OWN Travelling Roadshow began in Singapore, targeting three heartlands in Singapore: Yishun, Choa Chu Kang, and Bukit Panjang.

Singapore's first-ever “Coexistence Handbook” was launched to the public, offering comprehensive guidance on wildlife encounters, including detailed exclusion measures and insights into wildlife behaviour.
Our Wild Neighbours (OWN) Campaign was launched as a multi-stakeholder outreach programme to promote human-wildlife coexistence in Singapore.
Landmark repatriation of 51 Indian star tortoises back to the wild in India, along with partner NGO Wildlife SOS in India.
ACRES opens additional outdoor sanctuary to make room for tortoises to continue experiencing outdoor environment.
Innovative community wildlife rescue team is launched, with PDF advisories to seek help from public to help distressed wildlife upon our assessment and advise.
ACRES opens a brand new aviary for rehabilitation of rescued native species of birds before they can be returned to the wild.

Their first-ever reptile gets repatriated back to the wild, after being rescued from the illegal wildlife trade: Rahayu, a Malaysian giant turtle, an endangered species of freshwater turtle who was found on Singapore's roads with fish hook and line in her mouth.

2017 sees increase in the number of rescue calls totaling to over 27,000 calls (since 2010) to our hotline related to wildlife in distress; rescued over 14,000 wild animals and released over 7,000 wild animals back to the wild.
Action was taken against companies and individuals who flout the current laws of animal welfare and protection.
ACRES becomes a member of the python working group, which includes other members such as the Wildlife Reserves Singapore, NParks and the NUS.
A 6-month undercover investigation exposed over 150 prohibited wildlife being sold online in Singapore.
ACRES repatriates her first primate to Malaysia from the illegal wildlife trade in Singapore – a pig-tailed macaque.

ACRES becomes a member of the common palm civet and pangolin working group in Singapore, which was formed to consolidate efforts in conserving these animals in Singapore through rescue, education, rehabilitation and release along with the Nparks and WRS.
The advocacy work of ACRES was acknowledged by the Singapore Advocacy Awards, Singapore's first advocacy awards initiative.

First time repatriation of two Asian Leopard cats confiscated as a result of ACRES's investigations in 2018, due to joint efforts from National Parks Board, Wildlife Reserves of Singapore and the Malaysian Wildlife Department (Perhilitan).
Two turtles that had been caught in Singapore's illegal pet trade were successfully repatriated.

name: Animal Concerns Research and Education Society
